title: 第 26 節
en: Twentieth Khanda.
i. The hinkara is fire, the prastava air, the udgitha the sun, the pratihara the stars, the nidhana the moon. That is the Ra^ana Saman, as interwoven in the deities.

2. He who thus knows the Ra^ana, as interwoven in the deities, obtains the same world, the same happiness, the same company as the gods, he reaches the full life, he lives long, becomes great with children and cattle, great by fame. His rule is, ' Do not speak evil of the Brahma^as.'
Twenty-first Khanda.

i. The hinkara is the threefold knowledge, the prastava these three worlds, the udgitha Agni (fire), Vayu (air), and Aditya (sun), the pratihara the stars, the birds, and the rays, the nidhana the serpents, Gandharvas, and fathers. That is the Saman, as interwoven in everything.
2. He who thus knows this Saman, as interwoven in everything, he becomes everything.

II PRAPA77/A.KA, 22 KHAiVDA, 2. 3 3. And thus it is said in the following verse : ' There are the fivefold three (the three kinds of sacrificial knowledge, the three worlds &c. in their fivefold form, i.e. as identified with the hihkara, the prastava, &c), and the other forms of the Saman. Greater than these there is nothing else besides.'
4. He who knows this, knows everything. All regions offer him gifts. His rule is, ' Let him meditate (on the Saman), knowing that he is everything, yea, that he is everything1.'
Twenty-second Khanda2.

1. The udgitha, of which a poet said, I choose the deep sounding note of the Saman as good for cattle, belongs to Agni ; the indefinite note belongs to Pra^apati, the definite note to Soma, the soft and smooth note to Vayu, the smooth and strong note to Indra, the heron-like note to EWhaspati, the dull note to Varu^a. Let a man cultivate all of these, avoiding, however, that of Varu;za.

2. Let a man sing3, wishing to obtain by his song immortality for the Devas. ' May I obtain by my song oblations (svadha) for the fathers, hope for men, fodder and water for animals, heaven for the sacrificer, food for myself,' thus reflecting on these in his mind, let a man (Udgatrz priest) sing praises, without making mistakes in pronunciation, &c.
1 Here ends the Samopasana.
2 These are lucubrations on the different tones employed in singing the Saman hymns, and their names, such as vinardi, anirukta, nirukta, mndu .daksh;za, jlaksh?/a balavad, kraunXu, apadhvanta.
3 It would be better if the first ity agayet could be left out. The commentator ignores these words.
[3] D 34 A'flANDOGYA-UPANISHAD.

3. All vowels (svara) belong to Indra, all sibilants (ushman) to Pra^apati, all consonants (spar^a) to Mrityu (death). If somebody should reprove him for his vowels, let him say, ' I went to Indra as my refuge (when pronouncing my vowels) : he will answer thee.'

4. And if somebody should reprove him for his sibilants, let him say, ' I went to Pra^apati as my refuge : he will smash thee.' And if somebody should reprove him for his consonants, let him say, ' I went to Mrityu as my refuge : he will reduce thee to ashes.'

5. All vowels are to be pronounced, with voice (ghosha) and strength (bala), so that the Udgatrz may give strength to Indra. All sibilants are to be pronounced, neither as if swallowed (agrasta) *, nor as if thrown out (nirasta)2, but well opened3 (vivrzta), so that the Udgatrz may give himself to Pra^apati. All consonants are to be pronounced slowly, and without crowding them together 4, so that the Udgatrz may withdraw himself from Mrityu.
1 Grasa, according to the Rig-veda-pratuakhya 766, is the stiffening of the root of the tongue in pronunciation.
2 Nirasa, according to the Rig-veda-prati-yakhya 760, is the withdrawing of the active from the passive organ in pronunciation.

3 The opening, vivrz'ta, may mean two things, either the opening of the vocal chords (kha), which imparts to the ushmans their surd character (Rig. Prat. 709), or the opening of the organs of pronunciation (kara/za), which for the ushmans is aspr/sh/am sthitam (Rig. Prat. 719), or vivrz'ta (Ath. Prat. I, 31; Taitt. Prat. II, 5)- 4 Anabhinihita, for thus the commentaries give the reading, is explained by anabhinikshipta. On the real abhinidhana, see Rig. Prat. 393. The translation does not follow the commentary. The genitive pra§-apate^ is governed by paridadani.
ii prapa77/aka, 24 khanda, i. 35 Twenty-third Khanda.

1. There are three branches of the law. Sacrifice, study, and charity are the first 1, 2. Austerity the second, and to dwell as a BrahnW'arin in the house of a tutor, always mortifying the body in the house of a tutor, is the third. All these obtain the worlds of the blessed ; but the Brahmasawstha alone (he who is firmly grounded in Brahman) obtains immortality.

3. Pra^apati brooded on the worlds. From them, thus brooded on, the threefold knowledge (sacrifice) issued forth. He brooded on it, and from it, thus brooded on, issued the three syllables, Bhu/£, Bhuva/z, Sva/i.

4. He brooded on them, and from them, thus brooded on, issued the Om. As all leaves are attached to a stalk, so is all speech (all words) attached to the Om (Brahman). Om is all this, yea, Om is all this.
Twenty-fourth Khanda.

1. The teachers of Brahman (Veda) declare, as the Prata/z-savana (morning-oblation) belongs to the Vasus, the Madhyandina-savana (noon-libation) to 1 Not the first in rank or succession, but only in enumerating the three branches of the law. This first branch corresponds to the second stage, the a^rama of the householder. Austerity is meant for the Yanaprastha, the third a\srama, while the third is intended for the BrahmaMrin, the student, only that the naish///ika or perpetual BrahmaMrin here takes the place of the ordinary student. The Brahmasa7#stha would represent the fourth a^-rama, that of the Sannyasin or parivra^-, who has ceased to perform any works, even the tapas or austerities of the Vanaprastha.
D 2 2,6 tfffANDOGYA-UPANISHAD.

the Rudras, the third Savana (evening-libation) to A the Adityas and the Viive Devas, 2. Where then is the world of the sacrificer ? He who does not know this, how can he perform the sacrifice ? He only who knows, should perform it1.

3. Before the beginning of the Prataranuvaka (matin-chant), the sacrificer, sitting down behind the household altar (garhapatya), and looking towards the north, sings the Saman, addressed to the Vasus :
zh: 第二十明分
木聲（hinkara）是火，前誦（prastava）是氣，唱誦（udgitha）是太陽，應答（pratihara）是星辰，終頌（nidhana）是月亮。此乃羅沙那讚歌（Ra^ana Saman），如同交織於諸提婆之中者。
2. 如此認識融貫於諸神之中的羅那（Rayana）者，獲得與神相同的世界、相同的幸福、相同的伴侶，他達致圓滿的生命，長壽，以子嗣與牲畜而偉大，因名聲而偉大。他的法則是：「勿詆毀婆羅門（Brahmaṇas）。」
第二十一開章
興卡羅（hinkara）是三重知識，普拉斯塔瓦（prastava）是這三個世界，烏德基塔（udgitha）是阿格尼（火）、伐猶（air）和阿迪提亞（太陽），普拉蒂哈羅（pratihara）是眾星、飛鳥和光線，尼達納（nidhana）是蛇類、乾闥婆（Gandharvas）和祖先。這就是薩曼（Saman），如其交織於萬物之中。
2. 凡如此認識此三摩笈多（Saman）者，知其與萬物交織於一體，彼則成為萬物。
II 普拉帕塔卡（PRAPA77/A.KA），22 開達（KHANDA），2.3 3. 因此在下列頌句中如是說道：「有五重三分（三種祭祀知識、三個世界等等以其五重形式存在，即如同與欣卡拉（hihkara）、普拉斯塔瓦（prastava）等等相認同），以及頌讚的其他形式。除此之外沒有更大的東西。」
4. 知道這一點的人，知道一切。所有地域都向他獻禮。他的法則是：「讓他冥想（吠陀讚歌〔Saman〕），知道他就是一切，是的，他就是一切¹。」
第二十二明分 --- **註：** 原文未提供具體內容段落。如有完整的英文原文需要翻譯，請提供該段落的全文，將依照上述規則進行翻譯。
烏德吉塔（*udgitha*）——一位詩人曾說過「我選擇沙摩讚歌深沉有力的音調作為對牲畜有益的」——屬於阿格尼；不確定的音調屬於梵天（*Prajāpati*），確定的音調屬於蘇摩，柔和光滑的音調屬於風神（*Vayu*），光滑而有力的音調屬於因陀羅，蒼鷺般的音調屬於婆梨詞帝（*Bṛhaspati*），沉鈍的音調屬於伐樓那（*Varuṇa*）。人應當培養這一切音調，然而要避免伐樓那的音調。
2. 讓一個人歌唱，希望藉由他的歌聲為提婆獲得不朽。「願我藉由我的歌聲為祖先獲得祭品（svadha），為人類帶來希望，為動物提供飼料和水，為祭祀者帶來天堂，為我自己獲得食物，」如此在心中思考這些事，讓一個人（烏德嘎特里祭司）誦唱讚美詩，而不在發音等方面犯錯。
此處《自我崇拜》（Samopasana）告終。
這些是關於詠唱薩摩（Saman）讚歌所採用之不同音調及其名稱的著述，例如 vinardi、anirukta、nirukta、mdu·daksha、jlaksha、balavad、kraundu、apadhvanta。
3 如果能夠將第一個 ity agayet 刪去會更好。註釋者對這些詞語置之不理。
[3] D 34 《歌者奧義書》(CHANDOGYA-UPANISHAD)。
3. 所有元音（svara）屬於因陀羅，所有咝音（ushman）屬於梵天（Pra^apati），所有輔音（spar^a）屬於摩梨多（Mrityu，死亡）。如果有人應因其元音而責備他，他應當說：「我去尋求因陀羅作為我的庇護（在發音元音時）：他將回答你。」
4. 若有人因其咝音而責難他，他應當說，「我以普剌闍巴蒂（Pra^apati）為避難所：他將摧毀你。」若有人因其輔音而責難他，他應當說，「我以姆利都（Mrityu）為避難所：他將把你化為灰燼。」
5. 所有元音都應當以聲音（ghosha）和力度（bala）來發音，使得歌誦祭司（Udgatrz）得以將力量賦予因陀羅。所有的齒音都應當發音，既不如吞嚥般發音（agrasta），亦不如拋出般發音（nirasta），而當開口清晰（vivrzta），使得歌誦祭司得以將自身獻予梵天主（Pra^apati）。所有的輔音都應當緩慢發音，且不得倉促拼湊，使得歌誦祭司得以脫離死神（Mrityu）。
根據《梨俱吠陀讚頌經》（Rig-veda-pratuakhya）766條，格拉薩（Grasa）是指在發音時舌根的僵硬。
根據《梨俱吠陀語音論》760 條，「尼剌娑」(Nirasa) 乃發音時主動器官從被動器官之抽離。

3 「開放」（vivrz'ta）可以有兩種含義：要麼是聲帶（kha）的開放，它賦予濁音（ushmans）其清音特徵（《梨俱吠陀經文論》709），要麼是發音器官（kara/za）的開放，對於濁音而言乃為「不接觸的狀態」（aspr/sh/am sthitam）（《梨俱吠陀經文論》719），或開放（vivrz'ta）（《雅者吠陀經文論》第一卷第31；《泰迪利耶經文論》第二卷第5）— 4 「未直接奠定」（Anabhinihita），經文注疏即以此作為讀法，而被解釋為「未直接拋擲」（anabhinikshipta）。關於真正的「直接奠定」（abhinidhana），見《梨俱吠陀經文論》393。此譯文不遵循注疏。屬格形式「主人」（pra§-apate^）由「分配」（paridadani）一詞支配。
二、普拉帕塔卡 (prapa77/aka)，24 坎達，第一卷，第 35 節 第二十三坎達。
法有三個分支。祭祀、學習與慈善為第一種，禁慾為第二種，而在導師家中作為梵行者（Brahmacharya）居住，常以禁欲苦行克制身體在導師家中，乃為第三種。凡此種種皆獲得至福之世界；然惟有梵住者（Brahmasawstha）——即堅根於梵者——方能獲得不朽。
3. 原人主（Pra^apati）沈思於諸世界。由此沈思而生，三重知識（祭祀）應運而出。他沈思於此，由此沈思而生，遂發出三音節：「婆」（Bhu/£）、「婆相」（Bhuva/z）、「淨」（Sva/i）。
4. 他思量著它們，由他這樣思量著的它們，發出了「唵」(Om)。如同所有的葉子都依附於一根莖，一切言語（所有的言詞）也都依附於「唵」(Brahman)。「唵」就是這一切，是的，「唵」就是這一切。
第二十四章

吠陀的教師宣稱，如同晨間奉獻（Prata/z-savana）屬於瓦蘇神族（Vasus），正午奉獻（Madhyandina-savana）屬於1 不是按階級或繼承順序排列的第一位，而只是在列舉法律的三個分支時為首。這第一個分支對應於第二個階段，即家主的修行階段（a^rama）。苦行是為了林棲者（Yanaprastha）準備的，這是第三個修行階段（a\srama），而第三個分支則是為梵行學生（BrahmaMrin）而設，只是這裡的永恆梵行者（naish///ika或perpetual BrahmaMrin）取代了普通的學生。梵行沙斯陀派（Brahmasa7#stha）將代表第四個修行階段（a^-rama），即已離俗者（Sannyasin或parivra^-）的階段，此人已停止進行任何修為，甚至連林棲者（Vanaprastha）的苦行（tapas）也不再進行。
香‧昂多吉亞奧義書（Chhândogya-Upanishad）
魯陀羅眾神，第三獻祭時刻（晚間奠酒）敬獻於阿迪提亞眾神與毗婆提婆眾神。2. 那麼祭祀者的世界在何處呢？不知此者，如何能行祭祀？只有知者，才應行祭祀¹。
3. 在晨唱部分（Prataranuvaka）開始之前，祭祀者坐在家庭祭壇（garhapatya）後方，面朝北方，唱誦獻給提婆（Vasus）的頌歌（Saman）：
